model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The scheduled Adam setup already improved the short-budget run, so the next efficient change is regularization aimed directly at validation loss. Label smoothing should improve calibration and cross-entropy on CIFAR-10, while a small dropout rate is a lightweight companion regularizer for this tiny model.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last regularization-heavy step clearly hurt, so this candidate removes those changes and spends the short fixed-step budget on slightly more model capacity instead. A modest width increase is a simple, low-risk way to raise representation power without introducing deeper training dynamics or heavy compute overhead.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The strongest gain so far came from modestly increasing model capacity, which suggests the current network is still underpowered for the 256-step budget. This follows the same direction with one more conservative width increase, keeping the training recipe unchanged so the comparison isolates whether extra capacity continues to pay off.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The current model is still trained for only 256 optimizer steps, so it sees a relatively small fraction of CIFAR-10. Increasing batch size is a direct way to expose the model to substantially more examples within the same step budget, and the modest learning-rate bump keeps the optimization scale aligned with the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current larger-batch configuration is working well, so the next step is to keep the overall recipe intact and improve how regularization interacts with Adam-style updates. Switching to AdamW with a slightly stronger decoupled weight decay is a simple optimizer-family change that often yields better validation loss than coupled L2 at this model size and budget.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = "adam"","old":"OPTIMIZER = "adamw"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The capacity increases and larger batch both helped substantially, while the optimizer swap did not. This candidate restores the better Adam setup and tests whether one more residual block can use the stronger data exposure more effectively than another pure width change.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current configuration is already near the apparent runtime limit, so the next capacity increase should avoid touching the expensive convolutional trunk. Expanding only the small classifier head is a cheap way to test whether the improved backbone features are now bottlenecked by the projection into class logits.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increasing the classifier head from 64 to 128 helped again, which suggests the pooled representation is still being bottlenecked late in the network. Doubling the head once more is a cheap capacity test that should barely affect runtime compared with modifying the convolutional trunk.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier head has improved validation loss at both recent expansions while barely affecting runtime, so it remains the highest-signal low-cost direction. One more increase tests whether the current convolutional body is still underutilized by the final MLP bottleneck.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The last head expansion overshot slightly, so this candidate returns to the better 256-unit head and then relaxes weight decay a bit. At this stage the model is substantially larger than the baseline but still trained for only 256 steps, so slightly less regularization may let the stronger architecture fit useful signal faster without changing runtime.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current stronger model continues to improve with relatively aggressive optimization, and reducing weight decay helped it fit faster. A modest additional learning-rate increase is the simplest next probe for whether this configuration still benefits from moving through the fixed 256-step budget more aggressively.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase improved validation loss again, so the current model still appears to benefit from more aggressive optimization within the fixed 256-step budget. A further moderate increase is the simplest continuation of the strongest recent trend without adding runtime or structural complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model is still improving as regularization is relaxed, and the higher learning rate appears stable. A small additional reduction in weight decay is a low-complexity probe for whether this short-budget run is still slightly underfitting rather than overfitting.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The last step suggests the current regularization level was already near-optimal, so this candidate restores it and then targets optimizer responsiveness instead. With only 256 training steps, a lower Adam second-moment beta can help the optimizer adapt faster to the large-batch, high-learning-rate regime without changing runtime or architecture.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"Lowering Adam's second-moment beta helped slightly, which is consistent with this very short training horizon favoring more responsive optimizer statistics. A small additional reduction continues that trend while keeping the successful architecture, learning rate, and regularization unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The extra beta2 reduction hurt, so this candidate restores the better optimizer setting and spends the remaining search budget on one last learning-rate push. Recent results suggest the model is close to the edge of a useful high-LR regime, so a moderate increase is the highest-signal remaining probe.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The clearest remaining trend is that this architecture continues to improve as the learning rate is raised, and the current 3.5e-3 setting remained stable.
